core_takeaway: The first position looks advantaged because Agent_1 immediately claimed Quill, the highest-value item for them, and framed the rest of the negotiation around non-Quill stated needs. Agent_5 still did well by claiming Apple, but accepted the Quill anchor and ended 12 utility behind.
why_or_counterexample: Agent_1's first move set a hard Quill reservation and then converted the discussion into a fair-looking split of the remaining items. The counterpoint is that Agent_5 also secured their best non-Quill item, so the result is not simple exclusion; the first-position edge came from locking the more valuable focal item before anyone contested it.

phase_notes.public_discussion: Agent_1 opened by reserving Quill and asking others to name non-Quill priorities; later speakers accepted that frame, with Agent_5 claiming Apple.
phase_notes.private_thinking: Present for all five agents. Private notes describe a locked baseline, Agent_1 preserving dominant Quill, and Agent_5 aligning with Apple.
phase_notes.formal_proposals: All five formal proposals used the same allocation: Agent_1 Quill, Agent_2 Stone, Agent_3 Jewel, Agent_4 Pencil, Agent_5 Apple.
phase_notes.private_voting: All 25 private votes accepted. Voters justified acceptance by their allocated item being best, top non-Quill, or best attainable under the baseline.
phase_notes.reflection: Absent; no reflection entries are present in the transcript.

mechanisms.theme: First speaker anchored Quill as nonnegotiable
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1 used the opening position to reserve Quill, then repeated that commitment after hearing other signals. This made the final 51 utility outcome the default rather than an item others actively bargained over.

evidence.quote: I will keep Quill (my top item, value 51) this round.
Quill with me; Stone to Agent_2; Jewel to Agent_3; Apple to Agent_5; Pencil to Agent_4.
this baseline gives me Quill (51) in Round 1

mechanisms.explanation: The initial framing invited others to compete for Apple, Jewel, Stone, and Pencil while Quill stayed with Agent_1. Agents 2, 3, and 5 then identified non-Quill targets instead of challenging the first speaker's top item.

evidence.quote: My top priority among the non-Quill items: Stone (38).
My priorities among non-Quill items: Jewel first
My top non-Quill item: Apple (worth 39 to me).

mechanisms.theme: Identical proposals converted the anchor into consensus
mechanisms.explanation: Once the baseline formed, every agent submitted the same allocation. That eliminated proposal competition and made the first-position Quill claim part of a unanimous package.

evidence.quote: This baseline assigns Quill to Agent_1

mechanisms.theme: Private incentives favored accepting the settled split
mechanisms.explanation: Private reasoning shows agents viewed their assigned item as sufficient or optimal under the baseline. That reduced incentives to reopen Quill even when some valued it or nearby items.

evidence.quote: My own priority is to keep Quill, which dominates value (51).
My own high-value item is Stone (38)
My own top item is Apple (39)
Accepting avoids stalling and preserves momentum.

mechanisms.theme: Last-position counterpoint: Agent_5 was not shut out
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_5 used the last public turn to claim Apple and got 39 utility, so the first-over-last gap was not caused by denial of Agent_5's main stated target. The gap mainly reflects Agent_1 keeping a 51-value Quill while Agent_5's accepted Apple was worth 39.

evidence.quote: Apple is my top non-Quill item (worth 39 to me)
this proposal gives me 39 and matches the public baseline.
